text: The surgeon pulls up fibers and spreads them laterally to create space at the specified angle.
The surgeon manipulates tissue on the left crus, focusing on removing or adjusting structures in this area.

The surgeon inspects the left side for any bleeding and begins taking down the short gastric vessels. The esophagus is prepared for mobilization, with plans to open the hiatus and bring the esophagus down further.

The surgeon injects the esophagus with a solution of epinephrine, aiming to reduce bleeding and create a wheal that separates the veins slightly. A small vein is visible over the top of the injection site.

The surgeon begins spreading the muscle fibers, focusing on the upper region of the muscle where the fibers are visible. The mediastinum is open, and the CO₂ levels are rising, indicating a high risk during the procedure.
The surgeon identifies the z-line, a visible anatomical landmark, and discusses the need to move slightly further down, approximately another centimeter, to reach the desired location.
